driver.body: A sensor network is only as broad as the units Kepler can actually manufacture, land, and place. In **Devices**, the supply chain is therefore not a back-office function — it is the **throttle on how fast breadth (B) compounds** [S-073], and it is managed as such rather than as a logistics cost line. Every fab constraint, assembly bottleneck, and port chokepoint reads directly as a ceiling on how quickly the sensor mesh — the term that feeds the moat's breadth factor across the 42 cities — can actually widen.

mechanisms.body:
The physical flow runs from silicon to placement [S-509], and two structural choices along it are load-bearing [S-510]. First, **final assembly is concentrated at Jabil Penang**, with ASE Kaohsiung handling SoC packaging and test — deliberately dual-region against the fab base but single-node on final assembly, trading resilience for the tighter process control that a consistent sensor calibration demands. A sensor that reads differently unit-to-unit corrupts the data term (D) it is supposed to feed, so calibration discipline is allowed to outrank redundancy at this leg.

Second, the **placement leg is Kepler's own**. Units land at the PSA Singapore and Rotterdam hubs and then ride the D1 mobility fleet and D3 courier network into the field, rather than moving through third-party retail [S-509]. This is the **distribution coupling that a standalone device maker cannot replicate** — Kepler already has vehicles and couriers moving through all 42 cities daily, so the last mile of device placement is nearly free and rides infrastructure that already exists [S-510]. Distribution, for a normal hardware company the most expensive way to buy breadth, is here a fixed asset already paid for by two other domains.

component_data.caption: Silicon to street: dual-region fabs, single-node final assembly at Jabil Penang with ASE Kaohsiung packaging, landing at the PSA Singapore and Rotterdam hubs, then placed through Kepler's own D1 fleet and D3 couriers across 42 cities rather than third-party retail — the distribution coupling a standalone device maker cannot replicate [S-509][S-510].

mechanisms.body:
The ramp plots the KB-1 v2 and the embedded KN-2 across quarters from FY26 to FY27 [S-511], and the schedule makes one dependency unmissable [S-512]: **the KN-2 embedded module's mass production is gated on the D1 vehicle manufacturing line**, because the Node is built into vehicles at their point of assembly, not retrofitted afterward. This is the cross-domain coupling expressed as a schedule constraint — Devices cannot ramp its highest-breadth embedded sensor any faster than Mobility ramps the vehicles it lives inside.

The strategic reading is that **Devices' breadth-compounding is paced by D1's fleet growth**, which is precisely why sequencing — the governing thought — matters: place the sensor line and the vehicle line out of sync and the moat forms slower than the model requires [S-512]. Get them in phase and every vehicle rolling off D1's line ships a sensor into the field at zero incremental placement cost.

The Gantt surfaces a second edge case as well — the **concentration risk on Jabil Penang and the single Singapore port hub**. A typhoon-season disruption at Klang or Penang is a direct hit to breadth, not merely to margin, so the FY27 plan carries a **qualified second final-assembly node as insurance**, accepted as a margin cost paid explicitly against sensor-coverage continuity [S-512].
